questions.question: Differentiate between data hazards and control hazards in instruction pipelining, giving one example of each.
keyPoints: Data hazards occur when instructions depend on data produced by previous instructions still in pipeline
Control hazards arise from branch instructions that change program flow
Example of data hazard: RAW (Read After Write) when second instruction needs result from first
Example of control hazard: Branch instruction causing pipeline to fetch from wrong path

questions.question: Explain the difference between maskable and non-maskable interrupts (NMI) in computer systems.
keyPoints: Maskable interrupts can be enabled/disabled by the CPU using interrupt mask register
NMI cannot be ignored or masked by the CPU - always acknowledged
Maskable interrupts used for low-priority devices like keyboard, timer
NMI reserved for critical events like power failure, memory parity errors

questions.question: Explain the three types of data hazards (RAW, WAR, WAW) in instruction pipelines. Discuss two methods to resolve these hazards with their advantages and limitations.
keyPoints: RAW (Read After Write): True dependency - later instruction reads before earlier writes
WAR (Write After Read): Anti-dependency - later instruction writes before earlier reads
WAW (Write After Write): Output dependency - later instruction writes before earlier writes
Resolution methods: Operand forwarding (requires extra hardware, reduces stall cycles) and pipeline interlocks/stalling (simpler but reduces performance)

questions.question: Describe the interrupt-driven I/O process with reference to: (i) Interrupt vector table, (ii) Daisy-chain priority, and (iii) DMA vs Programmed I/O. Also explain how vectored interrupts improve interrupt service time.
keyPoints: Interrupt vector table contains addresses of ISR for each interrupt source - CPU directly jumps to correct handler
Daisy-chain priority: Devices connected in series, higher priority device closer to CPU, grant signal propagates through chain
DMA allows devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention, reducing CPU overhead
Vectored interrupts reduce latency by providing device-specific handler address directly, eliminating polling overhead
